title: Napply Ever After Limited v Commissioner of Customs & Border Control (Tax
text:
Napply Ever After Limited v Commissioner of Customs & Border Control (Tax
Appeal E535 of 2025) [2026] KETAT 73 (KLR) (27 March 2026) (Judgment)
Neutral citation: [2026] KETAT 73 (KLR)
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEAL TRIBUNAL
TAX APPEAL E535 OF 2025
RO OLUOCH, CHAIR, C WESONGA & E KOMOLO, MEMBERS
MARCH 27, 2026
BETWEEN
NAPPLY EVER AFTER LIMITED ........................................................ APPELLANT
AND
COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS & BORDER CONTROL .......... RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
Background
1. The Appellant is an individual taxpayer registered in Kenya whose principal activity manufacture,
dealing, and reselling of women’s hygiene and beauty products.
2. The Respondent is the principal ocer appointed under section 13 of the Kenya Revenue Authority
Act. The Kenya Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government of Kenya mandated with the duty
of collecting and receipting all tax revenue, and the administration and enforcement of all tax laws
set out in Parts 1& 2 of the First Schedule to the Act, for the purposes of assessing, collecting, and
accounting for all tax revenues in accordance with those laws.
3. The Appellant imported a consignment of menstrual panties on 8th May 2025 and classied them
under HS Code 9619.00.10. The Respondent, upon verication, reclassied the consignment to HS
Code 9619.00.90.
4. The Appellant lodged an objection to the tari classication decision and adduced the product photos,
commercial invoice, bill of lading, and commercial packing list.
5. On 23rd May 2025, the Respondent issued a tari ruling upholding the reclassication under HS Code
9619.00.90.
6. The Appellant, being aggrieved by the said decision, lodged this instant Appeal with the Tribunal as
a Notice of Appeal dated
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 1

The Appeal.
7. The Appellant, in its Memorandum of Appeal dated 28th May 2025, the Appellant raised the following
grounds of Appeal:
a. That the Respondent erred in law and fact in reclassifying the Appellant’s imported
Nappily Ever After Menstrual Panties (Import Entry 25CTSIM400016841) From Tari Code
9619.00.10 TO 9619.00.90.
b. That the Respondent erred in law and fact by relying only on General Interpretation Rules
(GIRs) 1 and 6 while disregarding General Interpretation Rule 3 which is the relevant
intervening rule leading to the misclassication of the Appellant’s Nappily Ever After
Menstrual Panties.
c. That the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to sequentially apply General
Interpretation Rules, choosing instead to rely on Rules 1 and 6.
d. That the Respondent failed the Appellant’s legitimate expectation when it sought to
reclassify the imported consignment of menstrual panties contrary to Section 4 of the Fair
Administration Action Act as read with Article 47 of the Kenyan Constitution.
Appellant’s Case
8. The Appellant led its Statement of Facts dated 28th December 2025 and its Written Submissions dated
25th July 2025 and led on 23rd January 2026.
9. That on 8th May 2025, the Appellant imported and rightfully classied a consignment of menstrual
panties, entry number 25CTSIM400016841 under HS Code 9619.00.10 in line with the East Africa
Community Customs External Tari (EAC CET), valued at Kshs. 1,315,165.57.
10. The Appellant stated that the Respondent, without evidence, wrongfully reclassied the imported
consignment of menstrual panties under HS Code 9619.00.90.
11. The Appellant submitted that it then lodged an objection to the said reclassication. The In the
said objection, it raised its grounds of objection and attached photos of the product, the commercial
invoice, the Bill of Lading, and the product packaging list.
12. That Appellant averred that the Respondent ignored its rights of legitimate expectation as provided
under Section 4 of the Fair Administrative Action Act and Article 47 of the Kenyan Constitution and
went ahead to issue a tari ruling on 23rd May 2025 upholding the reclassication code HS 9619.00.90.
13. The Appellant submitted that the Respondent erred in law and fact by premising their decision on
product uses rather than the actual product that was placed before them. That the Respondent was
misinformed and misplaced by attempting to describe the product uses as the basis for classication,
which was evident in the tari ruling where a lengthy but unsubstantiated product description,
characteristics, and use were proered.
14. The Appellant averred that classication is based on the product as laid before the Respondent, and
attempts by it to describe its uses would be acting ultra vires.
15. The Appellant sought to inform the Respondent that in classication matters, the GIRs are to be
applied sequentially and not at the convenience of the Respondent with the aim of swelling the tax
coers. That the GIRs, while not legally binding, are used for ease of tax administration.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 2

16. The Appellant stated that GIR 1 of the EAC CET provides as follows:
“The titles of Sections, Chapters, and sub-Chapters are provided for ease of reference only;
for legal purposes, classication shall be determined according to the terms of the headings
and any relative Section or Chapter Notes and, provided such headings or Notes do not
otherwise require, according to the following provisions.”
17. The Appellant additionally referenced GIR 2(b) which states:
“(b) Any reference in a heading to a material or substance shall be taken to include a
reference to mixtures or combinations of that material or substance with other
materials or substances. Any reference to goods of a given material or substance
shall be taken to include a reference to goods consisting wholly or partly of
such materials or substances. The classication of goods consisting of more
than one material or substance shall be according to the principles of Rule 3.”
18. The Appellant further stated that in this instant case, GIR 3 is relevant as the imported consignment
has duality in material composition. GIR 3 states:
“3 The heading which provides the most specic description shall be preferred to
(a) headings providing a more general description. However, when two or more
headings each refer to part only of the materials or substances contained in
mixed or composite goods or to part only of the items in a set put up for retail
sale, those headings are to be regarded as equally specic in relation to those
goods, even if one of them gives a more complete or precise description of the
goods.
(b) Mixtures, composite goods consisting of dierent materials or made up of
dierent components, and goods put up in sets for retail sale, which cannot
be classied by reference to 3(a), shall be classied as if they consisted of the
material or component which gives them their essential character, in so far as
this criterion is applicable.
(c) When goods cannot be classied by reference to 3(a) or 3(b), they shall be
classied under the heading which occurs last in numerical order among those
which equally merit consideration.
19. The Appellant further referenced GIR 6 and stated that it is important with regard to this instant
appeal. The rule states thus:
“For legal purposes, the classication of goods in the subheadings of a heading shall be
determined according to the terms of those subheadings and any related Subheading Notes
and, mutatis mutandis, to the above Rules, on the understanding that only subheadings
at the same level are comparable. For the purposes of this Rule, the relative Section and
Chapter Notes also apply, unless the context otherwise requires.”
20. The Appellant submitted that they are aware of the Respondent’s powers as couched under Section
236 of EACCMA; however, these powers are not to be used to jeopardize the business interests of
taxpayers.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 3

21. The Appellant asserted that, contrary to the Respondent’s ruling, the imported consignment of
menstrual panties nds appropriate classication under HS Code 9619.00.10 which clearly aligns with
the terms of the headings and relative Section and Chapter Notes as provided under GIR 1.
22. That additionally GIR 2 provides that a heading to a material or substance shall be taken to include a
reference to mixtures or combinations of that material or substance with other materials or substances;
and that is why even the Respondent agreed that the menstrual panties fall under Heading 9619.
23. The Appellant submitted that it is peculiar on the part of the Respondent to seek to deviate from a
code (9619.00.10) that clearly identies the Appellant’s product “Sanitary towels (pads) and tampons”
and to seek to reclassify it under a code (9619.00.90) that is both ambiguous and nonspecic, “Other.”
24. In buttressing this point, the Appellant relied on the law set out in Cape Brandy Syndicate v. Inland
Revenue Commissioner (1921) 1KB64 where the Court stated:
“In a taxing act, one has to look merely at what is clearly stated. There is no room for any
intendment. There is no equity about tax. There is no presumption as to tax. Nothing is to
be read in, nothing is to be implied. One can only look fairly at the language used.”
25. The Appellant contended that in attempting to reclassify the Appellant’s product, the Respondent
was attempting to rewrite classication rules and laws, thus playing judge, jury, and executioner at the
same time, contrary to the law in Republic v. Commissioner of Customs Ex Parte Mulchand Ramji
& Sons Limited [2010] eKLR which stated:
“To ascertain what code is applicable to a particular good, one has to look at the General
Interpretation Rules for the classication of goods, which set out the principles of
interpretation in conjunction with the various chapters describing the goods and duty due.”
26. The Appellant submitted that while EAC CET was derived from and informed by the International
Convention of the HS, to which Kenya is a signatory, GIRs provide the framework for classifying
goods in the nomenclature, which ensures consistency and accuracy in the classication process.
27. The Appellant averred that had the Respondent been keen in the administration and application of
classication laws, it would have arrived at the same tari classication as the Appellant, and that there
was enough jurisprudence that would have guided the Respondent in the matter.
28. The Appellant contended that the Respondent’s decision to selectively apply GIRs not only prejudiced
the Appellant’s legitimate expectation but had led to irreparable nancial loss in the form of demurrage
on the detained consignment that accrues on a daily basis.
29. The Appellant relied on the law as set out in the following authorities:
a. Beta Healthcare International Ltd vs. Commissioner for Customs Services (2020) eKLR
b. Premier Solar Solutions Limited vs. Commissioner of Customs and Border Control (TAT
Appeal No. 984 of 2020)
c. Switchgear Limited vs. Commissioner of Customs and Border Control [Appeal No. 637 of
2021]
30. The Appellant concluded by stating that the Respondent’s tari ruling dated 23rd May 2025 was
therefore not merited.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 4

31. The Appellant armed the above position in its written submissions dated 25th July 2025 and led
on 23rd January 2026.
Appellant’s Prayer
32. The Appellant prayed that the Tribunal issue the following orders: -
a. That the instant Appeal be allowed.
b. That the Appellant’s tari classication under code 9619.00.10 be found to be the proper and
appropriate classication.
c. That the Appellant’s goods be released pending the hearing and determination of this Appeal.
d. That the Respondent’s tari Review Decision dated 23rd May 2025 be set aside in its entirety.
e. That the costs of this Appeal be awarded to the Appellant.
Respondent's Case
33. The Respondent opposed this Appeal with the support of its Statement of Facts dated 24th June 2025
and led on 26th June 2025 and its Written Submissions dated 30th January 2026 and led on 2nd
February 2026.
34. The Respondent stated that the article presented before it was identiable as “disposable menstrual
pants”. That it is a single-use modern alternative to traditional menstrual products like pads and
tampons, composed of non-woven fabric, u pulp, SAP, PE lm, and an elastic band.
35. The Respondent further stated that the said article is specically designed for menstrual ow by being
absorbent and leak-proof and is designed to be comfortable like regular underwear. The article is
designed to be a snug t that prevents shifting and enables comfort during wear.
36. The Respondent submitted that Heading 96.19 covers sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, napkins
(diapers), napkin liners, and similar articles of any material.
37. The Respondent averred that the Explanatory Notes to this Heading further elaborate as follows:
“This heading covers sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, napkins (diapers) and napkin liners
for babies and similar articles, including absorbent hygienic nursing pads, napkins (diapers)
for adults with incontinence, and pantyliners of any material.
In general, the articles of this Heading are disposable. Many of these articles are composed of;
a. An inner layer (e.g. of nonwovens) designed to wick uid from the wearer’s
skin and thereby prevent chang;
b. An absorbent core for collecting and storing uid until the product can be
disposed of; and
c. An outer layer (e.g. of plastics) to prevent leakage of uid from the absorbent
core.
The articles of this heading are usually shaped so that they t snugly to the human body.
This heading also includes similar traditional articles made solely of textile materials, which
are usually reusable following laundering.”
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 5

38. The Respondent submitted that they equally relied on GIR 1 and 6 in determining the tari
classication. That GIR 1 provides as follows:
“The titles of Sections, Chapters, and sub-chapters are provided for ease of reference only;
for legal purposes, classication shall be determined according to the terms of the headings
and any relative Section or Chapter Notes and, provided such headings or Notes do not
otherwise require…”
39. The Respondent averred that the classication proposed by the Appellant does not make any reference
to menstrual panties, which according to the Appellant are synonymous with menstrual pads and
tampons. That whereas both pads and menstrual panties are used to manage menstrual ow, they dier
signicantly in design and functionality.
40. The Respondent posited that the product presented by the Appellant looks and feels like regular
underwear, oering a more comfortable and potentially discreet option as compared to menstrual pads
and tampons. That on the other hand, the pads have an adhesive backing, that sticks to the inside of
the underwear to stay in place.
41. The Respondent stated that sanitary pads are a disposable, external solution, while menstrual panties
(the product in question) provide an integrated solution to handling menstrual ow.
42. The Respondent contended that, based on the foregoing, the classication declared by the Appellant
was at variance with the tari ruling issued by the Respondent.
43. That the Respondent considered all the evidence and information presented to them before making
a ruling.
44. In its written submissions, the Respondent submitted that both parties correctly classied the product
under Heading 9619 which covers sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, napkins, and napkin liners for
babies and similar articles of any material.
45. That the point of departure was that the Appellant’s preferred HS Code 9619.00.10 specically
provides for sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, while the Respondent’s preferred HS Code
9619.00.90 “Other,” covers all the others not specically provided for under any subheading, and they
are napkins and napkin liners for babies and similar articles of any material.
46. The Respondent armed that out of the ve items covered under Heading 9619, two items have been
specically provided for under subheading HS Code 9619.00.10 that are sanitary towels (pads) and
tampons.
47. That Napkins and napkin liners for babies and similar articles of any material do not have a specic
subheading. Since menstrual panties have not been given a specic subheading, they will therefore fall
under the description of similar articles.
48. The Respondent contended that even though menstrual panties are used for the same purpose as pads,
they are not similar in nature and character.
49. The Respondent distinguished sanitary towels and menstrual panties as below:
Sanitary Towels Menstrual Panties
External absorbent hygiene products Garment-like products
Designed to be attached to undergarments Worn as underwear
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 6

Combine absorbent material,
Elastic waistbands and fabric structure
Do not function independently. Provide both coverage and
containment.
Requires a separate garment to function in the entire pelvic area
Limited to absorption only Function independently without
Classied internationally as sanitary articles additional clothing support.
Often resembles adult diapers in construction.
Manufacturing and Composition
Absorbent core Multi-layer construction:
Top sheet + back sheet Non-woven fabric
Adhesive strips Super-absorbent polymers (SAP)
No structural garment components Leak-proof back sheet
Elastic waist/leg cus
Closer to diapers/incontinence pants in design
50. The Respondent referenced the Explanatory notes in asserting that both sanitary towels (pads) and
menstrual panties are sanitary articles, but their structure, composition, and method of use dier
signicantly. That they do, however, fall under the same heading since they are both disposables.
51. The Respondent stated that "it was guided in its interpretation of the statute by the maxim that
Expressio Unius Est Exclusio Alterius," which means that where legislation specically mentions one
item in exclusion of another, the one left out was not meant to be there. That in the issue at hand, sub-
heading 9619.00.10 listed sanitary towels (pads") and placed in brackets "pads for clarity and avoidance
of doubt; the deliberate omission of menstrual panties indicates legislative intent not to include them.
52. The Respondent posited that courts have consistently held that what is expressly stated excludes what
is not, and tax law does not permit extension by analogy.
53. The Respondent averred that in ordinary trade and consumer understanding, sanitary towels (pads)
are hygiene consumables, while disposable menstrual pants are disposable clothing items.
54. That even if disposable menstrual pants serve a similar purpose as pads and fall under Heading 9619
as pointed out in the ENs that they are disposable, the two are distinct products, as demonstrated in
the preceding paragraphs above, and each product must independently meet the statutory denition
and classication.
55. The Respondent supported its case by relying on the law in Kimberley-Clark Ltd v. Commissioner of
Customs (EU) where the Court held that:
“Adult diapers with garment features were not sanitary towels. Classication depended on
structure and mode of wearing. Articles worn as garments cannot be classied as sanitary
pads merely because they absorb bodily uids.”
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 7

Respondent’s Prayer
56. The Respondent prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders:
a. That the Respondent’s ruling dated 23rd May 2025 was properly issued and should be upheld.
b. That the short-levied duty, inclusive of penalties and interest, be found due and payable.
c. That the Appeal herein be dismissed with costs to the Respondent.
Issues For Determination
57. The Tribunal has considered the parties' pleadings, documentation, and submissions and is of the
view that the only issue for determination in this appeal is: whether the Respondent was justied in
reclassifying the Appellant’s product from HS Code 9619.00.10 to HS Code 9619.00.90.
Analysis And Determination
58. Having identied the issue for determination in this Appeal, the Tribunal will proceed to analyze it
as follows.
59. The genesis of this Appeal is the Respondent’s tari review decision dated 23rd May 2025 re-classifying
the Appellant’s imported product from HS Code 9619.00.10 to HS Code 9619.00.90.
60. The Appellant argued that the product should be classied based on its presentation, which places it
under the code HS 9619.00.10. The Respondent, on the other hand, provides a detailed distinction of
the product, referencing its nature and essential character and distinguishing it from sanitary towels.
The Respondent argues that the product before it, which is a menstrual panty, should be classied
under HS Code 9619.00.90.
61. The Appellant submitted that tari code 9619.00.10 clearly provides for “Sanitary towels (pads) and
tampons” while the Respondent’s preferred tari code 9619.00.90 provides for “Other.” That the
consignment of menstrual panties, therefore, nds appropriate classication under Code 9619.00.10
which aligns with the terms of the headings and relative section and chapter notes as provided under
General Interpretative Rules 1 and backed by Rules 2.
62. The Respondent, on the other hand, is clear that Code 9619.00.10 refers to the mentioned items,
which are sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, and any other qualifying item that is not expressly stated
will automatically nd a home under Code 9619.00.90.
63. In determining customs classication matters, the Tribunal is guided by the East African Community
Common External Tari 2022(CET), General Interpretative Rules (GIRs) and the Explanatory Notes
to the Harmonized System.
64. Rule 1 of the GIRs states that:
“The titles of sections, chapters, and sub-chapters are provided for ease of reference only;
for legal purposes, classication shall be determined according to the terms of the headings
and any relevant section or Chapter Notes and, provided such headings or notes do not
otherwise require…”
65. This rule provides that the classication of goods should rst be determined by the headings (titles
of sections, chapters, and sub-chapters) in the Harmonized System. It emphasizes that the titles of
the sections, chapters, and sub-chapters are used for general guidance, but they are not necessarily
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 8

determinative of classication. The correct classication should be based on the actual content of the
headings and any applicable notes.
66. The Tribunal has reviewed the Section and Chapter Headings and sub-chapters in the CET, which
states as indicated below:
9619 Sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, napkins (diapers), napkin liners, and similar
articles, of any material.
9619.00.10--- Sanitary towels (pads) and tampons
9619.00.90--- Other
67. There is no dispute between the parties on the Heading 9619. The dispute is at the level of the sub-
headings with the Appellant preferring 9619.00.10 while the Respondent proers that the correct
classication is 9619.00.90.(Emphasis ours)
68. The Tribunal has looked at the Explanatory Notes to the HS for Chapter 96 Heading 16 which states
as follows:
“
“96. 16 – Sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, napkins (diapers), napkin liners, and
similar articles.
This heading covers sanitary towels (pads) and tampons, napkins (diapers) and napkin
liners, and similar articles, including absorbent hygienic nursing pads, napkins (diapers) for
adults with incontinence, and pantyliners, of any material.
In general, the articles of this heading are disposable. Many of these articles are composed of
(a) an inner layer (e.g. of nonwovens) designed to wick uid from the wearer’s skin
and thereby prevent chang;
(b) an absorbent core for collecting and storing uid until the product can be
disposed of; and
(c) an outer layer (e.g. of plastics to prevent leakage of uid from the absorbent
core. The articles of this heading are usually shaped so that they may t snugly
to the human body. This heading also includes similar traditional articles made
up solely of textile materials, which are usually reusable following laundering.
This heading does not cover products such as disposable surgical drapes and absorbent pads
for hospital beds, operating tables, and wheelchairs, or non-absorbent nursing pads or other
non-absorbent articles (in general, classied according to their constituent material).”
69. The Tribunal has carefully considered the Explanatory Notes to Heading 9619 and observes that the
heading broadly covers sanitary towels (pads), tampons, napkins (diapers), napkin liners, and similar
absorbent hygienic articles of any material. The Explanatory Notes further clarify that such products
generally consist of an inner layer designed to wick uid, an absorbent core, and an outer layer to
prevent leakage, and that they are typically shaped to t the human body. The Tribunal notes that the
Appellant’s product, described as menstrual panties, possesses absorbent layers intended to manage
menstrual ow and prevent leakage and therefore falls squarely within the general description of
absorbent sanitary articles contemplated under Heading 9619.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 9

70. The Tribunal further notes that the dispute between the parties does not concern the applicable
heading but rather the appropriate subheading. While the Respondent contends that the product
should fall under HS Code 9619.00.90 (“Other”), the Appellant maintains that the appropriate
classication is HS Code 9619.00.10 (“Sanitary towels (pads) and tampons”).
71. In resolving disputes relating to tari classication, the Tribunal is guided by the General Interpretative
Rules (GIRs) which must be applied sequentially. This position has been consistently armed by the
Tribunal in Premier Solar Solutions Limited v. Commissioner of Customs and Border Control, [2025]
KEHC 12782 (KLR) where it was held that classication must follow the interpretative hierarchy
set out in the GIRs and cannot be selectively applied. The Tribunal in that case emphasized that the
starting point is the wording of the heading and the relevant notes.
72. The Tribunal notes that both parties correctly relied on GIR 1, which requires classication to be
determined according to the terms of the headings and any relevant Section or Chapter Notes. The
Respondent further relied on GIR 6 in determining the applicable subheading. However, the Tribunal
observes that where goods may prima facie fall under more than one possible description, GIR 3
becomes relevant in determining the most appropriate classication.
73. The Appellant argued that the Respondent failed to apply GIR 3 despite the product being
a composite article comprising dierent materials, including non-woven fabric, super absorbent
polymer, and plastic layers. The Tribunal agrees that where goods consist of multiple materials or
components, the classication must be guided by the essential character of the product pursuant to
GIR 3(b).
74. The Tribunal has considered the functional attributes of the product and notes that its essential
character is the absorption and containment of menstrual ow. This is the same essential function
performed by sanitary pads and tampons. The Tribunal is therefore persuaded that the functional
purpose of the product aligns with sanitary hygiene articles designed for menstrual management.
75. The Tribunal has previously emphasized the importance of examining the essential character and
functional purpose of a product in tari classication. In Beta Healthcare International Ltd v.
Commissioner for Customs Services, [2012] KEHC 4527 (KLR) the Tribunal held that classication
must take into account the principal function and commercial identity of the goods as presented at
importation.
76. Similarly, in Switchgear Limited v. Commissioner of Customs and Border Control, (Appeal 637 of
2021) [2023] KETAT 129 (KLR) the Tribunal stated that the correct classication must reect the
product’s functional identity as understood in trade and commerce rather than a narrow interpretation
of structural characteristics.
77. Applying the foregoing principles, the Tribunal observes that although the product is designed in
the form of underwear, its essential purpose is not clothing but menstrual hygiene management. The
absorbent layers embedded in the product are specically engineered to perform the same function as
sanitary pads.
78. The Tribunal further notes that the Respondent’s argument focused heavily on the structural
characteristics of the product as resembling a garment. While this distinction is acknowledged, the
Tribunal nds that the presence of garment-like features does not negate the sanitary function of the
product. The determining factor remains the essential purpose of the article as a menstrual hygiene
product.
https://new.kenyalaw.org/akn/ke/judgment/ketat/2026/73/eng@2026-03-27 10

79. In this regard, the Tribunal is guided by the principle articulated in Republic v. Commissioner
of Customs Services ex parte Mulchand Ramji & Sons Ltd, [2010] eKLR where the High Court
emphasized that tari classication must be undertaken by reference to the General Interpretative
Rules and the commercial identity of the goods.
80. The Tribunal also nds persuasive the principle set out in Cape Brandy Syndicate v. Inland Revenue
Commissioners, [1921] 2 KB 403 which underscores that tax statutes must be interpreted strictly and
that nothing should be implied beyond the clear words of the law.
81. Applying this principle, the Tribunal notes that subheading 9619.00.10 specically provides for
sanitary towels and tampons, which are articles designed for menstrual hygiene management. While
menstrual panties are not expressly named, they perform the same essential hygienic function of
absorbing menstrual ow.
82. The Tribunal therefore nds that the Respondent adopted an unduly restrictive interpretation of
the subheading by focusing exclusively on the structural form of the product rather than its essential
sanitary function.
83. The Tribunal is further persuaded that the classication proposed by the Respondent under
subheading 9619.00.90 (“Other”) should only be resorted to where no more specic classication is
available. This principle was armed in Premier Solar Solutions Limited v. Commissioner of Customs
and Border Control, [2025] KEHC 12782 (KLR) where the Tribunal held that residual categories
must only be applied as a last resort.
84. In the present case, the Tribunal nds that the Appellant’s product shares the essential hygienic
characteristics of sanitary pads and therefore aligns more closely with the specic description under
HS Code 9619.00.10.
85. The Tribunal, therefore, concludes that the Respondent erred in reclassifying the Appellant’s goods
under HS Code 9619.00.90 without suciently considering the functional characteristics and
essential purpose of the product.
86. Consequently, the Tribunal nds that the Appellant’s classication under HS Code 9619.00.10 is
more consistent with the General Interpretative Rules and the functional identity of the product as
a menstrual hygiene article.
Final Decision
87. In light of the foregoing analysis, the Tribunal makes the following orders:
a. The Appeal be and is hereby allowed.
b. The Respondent’s tari ruling dated 23rd May 2025 reclassifying the Appellant’s goods under
HS Code 9619.00.90 is hereby set aside.
c. Each party shall bear its own costs.
88. It is so ordered.
DATED AND DELIVERED AT NAIROBI THIS 27TH DAY OF MARCH 2026
DR. RODNEY ODHIAMBO OLUOCH - CHAIRPERSON
